bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: gin Routing — Routes, Parameters, and Group Prefixes
bytes.body:
gin's router is built on httprouter, giving you zero-allocation, radix-tree-based routing. Define routes directly on the engine: `r.GET("/users", handler)`, `r.POST("/users", handler)`. Route parameters use the `:param` syntax — `r.GET("/users/:id", handler)` — and are retrieved via `c.Param("id")`. Wildcard segments use `*param`, matching everything including slashes: `/files/*path`.

Grouping routes with `r.Group("/api/v1")` returns a `*RouterGroup`, letting you attach a shared prefix and middleware to related endpoints without repetition. Middleware applied to a group only runs for that group's routes — not the full engine. Nest groups further for versioned APIs: a `/api` group containing a `/v1` subgroup.

Use `r.Any()` to register all HTTP methods on one path, or `r.Handle(method, path, handlers)` for dynamic method strings. Named routes aren't supported natively — if you need reverse routing, manage path constants yourself.

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Gin Project Structure — MVC vs Vertical Slice and When to Switch
bytes.body: The default instinct with Gin is MVC: split into `handlers/`, `services/`, `models/`, `routes/`. This works fine at small scale, but as features grow you end up chasing a single user-registration flow across four directories. Vertical slice flips this — group by feature, not layer. A `features/auth/` folder owns its handler, service, repository, and DTOs. Your `router.go` just calls `auth.RegisterRoutes(r)`, where each feature exposes a `RegisterRoutes(rg *gin.RouterGroup)` function. Middleware stays global in `middleware/` since it's truly cross-cutting. Gin's `RouterGroup` is the key primitive here — each feature receives its own group and mounts its own routes, keeping coupling minimal. For a `RouterGroup`-per-feature pattern, pass `*gin.Engine` into a bootstrap function and let each package call `engine.Group("/auth")`. Repository interfaces live inside each feature package, not a shared `db/` folder — this enforces boundaries and makes extraction to a microservice a folder-move, not a refactor. Stick with MVC until you hit ~5 features; migrate slice-by-slice after that.

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Gin Middleware — Pipeline Order, Global vs Route-Specific
bytes.body:
Gin middleware is just a `HandlerFunc` that calls `c.Next()` to pass control down the chain — or `c.Abort()` to short-circuit it. The order you register middleware is the order it executes, both before and after `c.Next()`. Global middleware registered via `r.Use()` runs on every request. Route-specific middleware is scoped by attaching it directly to a route group or individual route.

A common mistake: registering a logger after an auth middleware means unauthenticated requests never get logged. Register your logger first. Similarly, `gin.Recovery()` should always be the outermost middleware so it catches panics from everything downstream, including other middleware.

Group-scoped middleware is powerful for applying auth only to protected routes without polluting public ones. The middleware stack for a request is the engine-level stack plus the group stack plus the route-level handlers — all merged into a single `HandlersChain` slice. Gin executes them sequentially via index, so code after `c.Next()` in an outer middleware runs after all inner handlers complete — giving you natural pre/post hooks.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Gin DI — Manual Wiring vs Containers and What It Means for Testability
bytes.body:
Gin ships with no DI container. That's a feature, not a gap. The idiomatic pattern is closure-based handler factories: your handler function closes over its dependencies, keeping the gin.HandlerFunc signature clean. This pays off immediately in tests — swap a real `UserRepository` for a mock struct and call the handler directly, no HTTP stack required.

Where teams go wrong is reaching for global state (`var db *gorm.DB` at package level) to avoid the wiring ceremony. This makes unit tests impossible without side effects and kills parallelism.

For larger projects, Google Wire or Uber's dig compose the dependency graph at compile/startup time without runtime reflection overhead. Wire generates explicit Go code you can read; dig resolves by type at runtime. Both integrate cleanly with Gin: build your service graph first, then pass leaf dependencies into your router setup via `SetupRouter(svc *UserService) *gin.Engine`.

The rule: keep `gin.HandlerFunc` as a thin shell. Push logic into injected services. Your handler becomes a one-liner that calls a service method — trivially testable, trivially swappable.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Gin + GORM — Squashing N+1 Queries and Choosing the Right Database Layer
bytes.body:
Gin handlers don't care which database layer you use, but the choice shapes your entire codebase. GORM is the dominant full ORM in gin projects — it handles migrations, associations, and hooks, but at the cost of SQL transparency. sqlx sits at the other end: raw SQL with struct scanning, full control, no magic. sqlc goes further — write SQL, get type-safe Go structs generated at build time, zero reflection overhead.

The N+1 trap hits hardest with GORM. A handler that fetches posts and loops to load comments fires one query per post. Fix it with Preload: `db.Preload("Comments").Find(&posts)` emits exactly two queries regardless of result size — one for posts, one for comments using an IN clause.

For migrations, never rely on AutoMigrate in production — it won't drop columns or handle multi-step changes safely. Use golang-migrate with versioned .sql files and run migrations as an explicit step in your GitHub Actions workflow before the gin server container starts.

If query performance is critical, profile with GORM's built-in logger set to `logger.Info` mode — it prints every SQL statement and execution time directly to your gin server output.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Gin Input Validation — Binding, Sanitization, and Error Responses at the Boundary
bytes.body:
Gin's `ShouldBindJSON` (or `ShouldBind` for form data) is your validation boundary. Pair it with `go-playground/validator` tags directly on your request struct — `binding:"required,email,max=255"` — and Gin wires validation automatically on bind. Never trust raw input past this layer.

When `ShouldBindJSON` returns an error, cast it to `validator.ValidationErrors` to extract field-level failures and map them to human-readable messages. Return structured JSON errors — never expose raw validator output to clients.

For sanitization, strip HTML before persisting: use `microcosm-cc/bluemonday` after binding succeeds. Don't sanitize in the struct; do it explicitly in your handler or a thin service layer so the boundary stays clear.

Avoid `c.BindJSON` — it writes a `400` automatically on failure, robbing you of control over the error shape. `ShouldBindJSON` gives you full control. Register a custom `ErrorHandler` middleware to normalize all validation errors into a consistent `{ errors: [{field, message}] }` envelope across every route.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: gin Auth Middleware — JWT Validation and Context Propagation
bytes.body:
gin's middleware system makes JWT validation clean: register once on a RouterGroup, and every downstream handler inherits the auth context. Use gin.Context.Set/Get to pass parsed claims downstream — avoid re-parsing the token in each handler.

The key pattern is a custom gin.HandlerFunc that calls c.Abort() on failure and c.Next() on success. Always call c.AbortWithStatusJSON() instead of just c.Abort() so the response is written immediately and no further handlers execute.

For session handling, store only the user ID in the JWT claims and re-fetch the full user from cache (Redis) or DB in a separate middleware layer — don't trust mutable fields like roles directly from the token. This lets you revoke sessions server-side without waiting for token expiry.

Group protected routes under a single RouterGroup with the middleware applied: r.Group("/api").Use(AuthMiddleware()). Public routes stay on the root engine or a separate group — no accidental auth bypass from missed per-route registration.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Gin Error Handling — Global Middleware for Consistent Error Responses
bytes.body: Gin doesn't have a built-in global error handler, but you can build one using a custom middleware that intercepts errors set via c.Error() and formats them uniformly before the response is written. Call c.Error(err) anywhere in your handler chain — Gin appends it to c.Errors — then a single middleware at the end of the chain reads c.Errors.Last() and writes a structured JSON response. This keeps error-shaping logic out of individual handlers entirely. Define a canonical error type that carries an HTTP status, machine-readable code, and human message. Your middleware maps that type via errors.As(); anything unrecognized falls back to 500. Register the middleware after your routes with router.Use() or inline it as the last handler in a group. Pair this with Gin's c.AbortWithError() when you need to short-circuit the chain — it sets the status and appends to c.Errors in one call, so the middleware still owns the response body. The result: every error surface in your API returns the same JSON shape without duplicating response logic.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Gin Testing — Unit vs Integration, Mocking HTTP, and Test Database Strategy
bytes.body:
Gin's `gin.New()` vs `gin.Default()` matters in tests: skip the logger/recovery middleware noise by using `gin.New()` directly. For unit tests, isolate handler logic by calling it with a synthetic `*gin.Context` constructed via `httptest.NewRecorder()` and `http.NewRequest()`. For integration tests, spin up the full router with `gin.SetMode(gin.TestMode)` and drive it through `httptest.NewServer` or directly via `router.ServeHTTP(w, req)`.

For mocking downstream HTTP calls, inject an `*http.Client` into your service and swap it with `httptest.NewServer` returning canned responses — no external dependencies needed. Avoid global state in handlers; constructor injection makes this trivial.

For database strategy, use a separate test schema with `testcontainers-go` spinning up a real Postgres instance. Run migrations before each suite via `golang-migrate`. Never mock the DB layer in integration tests — Gin's real value is in the full request→handler→DB roundtrip. Use `t.Cleanup` to truncate tables between cases, not between suites.
bytes.code_snippet:
func TestCreateUser_Integration(t *testing.T) {
	gin.SetMode(gin.TestMode)
	router := gin.New()
	router.POST("/users", handlers.CreateUser(db))

	body := `{"name":"alice","email":"alice@example.com"}`
	w := httptest.NewRecorder()
	req := httptest.NewRequest(http.MethodPost, "/users", strings.NewReader(body))
	req.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")

	router.ServeHTTP(w, req)

	assert.Equal(t, http.StatusCreated, w.Code)
	assert.Contains(t, w.Body.String(), "alice")
}

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: gin Security Essentials — CORS, Rate Limiting, and Headers in One Setup
bytes.body: gin ships with no security defaults — you own the hardening. Start with `cors` via `github.com/gin-contrib/cors`: use `cors.New(cors.Config{AllowOrigins: []string{"https://yourdomain.com"}, AllowMethods: []string{"GET", "POST"}, AllowHeaders: []string{"Authorization", "Content-Type"}})` — never `cors.Default()` in production, which wildcards origins. Rate limiting belongs on the router group level using `github.com/ulule/limiter/v3` with its gin middleware; attach it before auth so unauthenticated flood is dropped early. Security headers aren't built-in — add them via a custom middleware that sets `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`, and `Strict-Transport-Security` on every response. For injection prevention, never interpolate route params directly into SQL — always bind to structs with `ShouldBindJSON` and validate with `go-playground/validator` tags like `binding:"required,alphanum"`. gin's `gin.SetMode(gin.ReleaseMode)` also disables debug stack traces in responses, a common info-leak vector.

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Gin Performance — Connection Pooling, Async Handlers, and gzip Compression
bytes.body:
Gin doesn't manage a database connection pool itself — that's your `*sql.DB` or GORM's underlying driver. Set `SetMaxOpenConns`, `SetMaxIdleConns`, and `SetConnMaxLifetime` once at startup and share the instance across handlers via `gin.Context` or dependency injection. Never open a new connection per request.

For async work inside a handler, copy the context with `c.Copy()` before spawning a goroutine. Accessing the original `*gin.Context` after the handler returns causes a data race since Gin recycles it into its sync.Pool.

Response compression is handled by `github.com/gin-contrib/gzip`. Wrap your router group with `gzip.Gzip(gzip.DefaultCompression)` — this transparently compresses responses when the client sends `Accept-Encoding: gzip`. Avoid wrapping health-check or metrics routes; compression overhead outweighs benefit on tiny payloads. Combine with `c.Header("Cache-Control", "public, max-age=3600")` on static API responses to cut repeat bandwidth. Together these three levers — pool tuning, safe goroutine dispatch, and selective compression — cover the most common Gin performance gaps in production.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Gin API Versioning — Route Groups, Middleware, and Swagger in One Setup
bytes.body:
Gin's `RouterGroup` is the cleanest primitive for REST versioning. Wrap each version in its own group and attach version-specific middleware without polluting the global chain.

Use `gin.New()` over `gin.Default()` in production — you control exactly which middleware runs. Attach `gin.Logger()` and `gin.Recovery()` explicitly, then layer version groups on top.

For OpenAPI generation, `swaggo/swag` integrates directly with Gin via `ginSwagger` and `swaggerFiles`. Annotate handlers with `// @Router /v1/users [get]` doc comments, run `swag init`, and mount the UI at `/swagger/*any` inside a non-versioned group so it doesn't inherit version middleware.

Keep `/v1` and `/v2` groups under a shared `/api` parent group — this lets you attach rate limiting or auth middleware once at the `/api` level. Never version at the subdomain level unless you control DNS; path versioning is simpler to route in Gin and easier to document with swag.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Gin Configuration — Env Vars, Structs, and Secrets Done Right
bytes.body:
Gin reads `GIN_MODE` natively — set it to `release` in production to silence debug logs and disable the colorized logger. Beyond that, Gin has no built-in config system, so pair it with `github.com/spf13/viper` or a plain struct loaded from env vars.

The idiomatic pattern is a typed `Config` struct populated via `os.Getenv` or `envconfig`, then injected into your router setup — never accessed globally mid-handler. Store secrets (DB passwords, API keys) in environment variables only; never in config files committed to source control. Use `.env` files locally with `godotenv.Load()` called before `viper.AutomaticEnv()`, so environment always wins over file values.

For `gin.Engine` itself, set `router.TrustedProxies` explicitly — leaving it as the default `["0.0.0.0/0"]` in production is a security hole. Combine config loading with `gin.SetMode` early in `main()`, before any middleware or route registration, so debug-only middleware like `gin.Logger()` can be conditionally attached based on the loaded config.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: gin — Structured Logs, Correlation IDs, and Request Middleware
bytes.body:
gin's default `gin.Logger()` middleware writes unstructured text — useless for log aggregators like Loki or Datadog. Replace it with a custom `gin.HandlerFunc` that injects a correlation ID and emits structured JSON on every request.

Use `c.Set("request_id", id)` to attach the ID to the context, then retrieve it downstream with `c.GetString("request_id")` in any handler or service call. Pair this with `log/slog` (Go 1.21+) or `zerolog` to emit structured fields.

Key fields to log: `request_id`, `method`, `path`, `status`, `latency`, and `client_ip` (via `c.ClientIP()`). Read latency by capturing `time.Now()` before calling `c.Next()`, then diffing after. For inbound correlation IDs from upstream services, check `c.GetHeader("X-Request-ID")` first and fall back to a fresh UUID — this preserves trace continuity across service boundaries. Register the middleware with `r.Use(RequestLogger())` before any route groups so every route is covered.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Gin Serialization — DTOs, snake_case, and Null Handling Done Right
bytes.body:
Gin relies entirely on Go's `encoding/json` and `github.com/go-playground/validator`, meaning your struct tags do double duty: controlling both JSON key names and validation rules.

For snake_case on the wire, annotate every field with `json:"snake_name"`. Without it, Go's exported PascalCase fields serialize as-is — `FullName` becomes `"FullName"` not `"full_name"`. Consistent casing between frontend and backend starts here.

Null handling has two modes. For optional response fields, use `omitempty` — `json:"avatar_url,omitempty"` drops the key entirely when the value is zero. For request fields that must be nullable (distinct from "not provided"), use a pointer like `*int`. A nil pointer serializes to JSON `null`; with `omitempty` added, it disappears entirely when nil.

Prefer `ShouldBindJSON` over `BindJSON` for request parsing. `BindJSON` writes a 400 and calls `c.Abort()` on error — you lose ownership of the error shape. `ShouldBindJSON` returns the error and lets you respond with your own consistent error envelope via `gin.H`.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Gin Dev Tooling — Hot Reload with Air and OpenAPI Docs with Swag
bytes.body:
Two tools every Gin developer should have wired in from day one: `air` and `swag`.

`air` watches your Go source files and rebuilds/restarts your Gin server on save — no manual `go run main.go` cycles. Drop an `.air.toml` at your repo root, set `cmd = "go build -o ./tmp/main ."` and `bin = "./tmp/main"`, and run `air`. It respects your build tags and handles `gin.SetMode(gin.DebugMode)` cleanly.

`swag` generates OpenAPI 3.0 spec and a Swagger UI directly from annotations on your Gin handler functions. Annotate your handlers with `// @Summary`, `// @Router /users [get]`, `// @Success 200 {object} UserResponse`, then run `swag init`. Wire the generated UI into Gin with `ginSwagger.WrapHandler` — your API is self-documenting with zero extra maintenance.

For scaffolding, `gonew` (Go 1.22+) can bootstrap a Gin project from a template module. Pair it with a `Makefile` that runs `swag init && air` in one command for a tight inner dev loop.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Gin Background Jobs — Goroutine Workers, Task Queues, and Scheduled Tasks
bytes.body:
Gin has no built-in job system, so background work lives alongside your router in `main()`. The safest pattern: spin up worker goroutines before calling `r.Run()`, communicating via buffered channels. Never pass `*gin.Context` directly into a goroutine — call `c.Copy()` first, since the context is reused by the pool after the handler returns.

For task queues, pair Gin with `asynq` (Redis-backed): enqueue tasks inside handlers using `client.Enqueue()`, then run `asynq.NewServer()` in a separate goroutine. This keeps your HTTP layer decoupled from processing.

For scheduled jobs, `robfig/cron` integrates cleanly — instantiate the scheduler before `r.Run()`, register jobs with `c.AddFunc("@every 1h", handler)`, and call `c.Start()`. Both the cron scheduler and Gin share the same process, so coordinate shutdown with a `context.WithCancel` and `os.Signal` listener calling `srv.Shutdown()` and `c.Stop()` in sequence. Avoid `go func()` fire-and-forget — unrecovered panics in detached goroutines will silently kill your worker.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: gin Monitoring — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and Distributed Tracing
bytes.body:
gin integrates cleanly with observability tooling through middleware and route composition. For health checks, register a dedicated `/health` route returning structured JSON — include DB ping, cache reachability, and uptime. Use `gin.Engine`'s route grouping to keep `/internal/*` endpoints separate from public routes, optionally protected by a middleware that checks a shared secret header.

For Prometheus metrics, `github.com/penglongli/gin-metrics` wraps `prometheus/client_golang` and attaches as a single `Use()` call, exposing `/metrics` automatically. It tracks request count, latency histograms, and in-flight requests per route pattern — all labeled with `gin`'s `FullPath()` rather than the raw URL, so `/users/123` and `/users/456` collapse into `/users/:id`.

For distributed tracing, `go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/github.com/gin-gonic/gin/otelgin` injects an OTEL span per request. Call `otelgin.Middleware("service-name")` before your business middleware. Downstream calls that use the request context automatically inherit the trace. Extract `trace_id` from the span and inject it into gin's response headers for client-side correlation.

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Gin's Built-In Batteries: What Ships vs. What You Wire Yourself
bytes.body:
Gin is deliberately minimal compared to Django's all-in-one philosophy or FastAPI's auto-generated OpenAPI docs. What gin.Default() gives you out of the box: Logger and Recovery middleware — that's it. No ORM, no schema introspection, no decorator magic like NestJS's @Get()/@Body().

Where gin earns its keep is struct binding with go-playground/validator tags. Call c.ShouldBindJSON(&req) and gin parses + validates in one shot — binding:"required" and validate:"email" live on the struct, not in route handlers. This is gin's answer to FastAPI's Pydantic models: less automatic, but explicit and zero-reflection overhead at runtime.

Route groups give you NestJS-style controller scoping without decorators. v1 := r.Group("/api/v1") lets middleware, auth, and versioning compose cleanly. For OpenAPI docs, you reach for swaggo/swag — gin has no equivalent to FastAPI's /docs endpoint. The tradeoff is intentional: gin stays fast by shipping sharp primitives, not a full framework stack. You own the assembly.
